ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 represents satay as a category; no single-stick portion code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKK covers grilled skewered meat broadly; no Singapore-specific single-stick satay code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for satay or grilled skewered meat as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One satay stick weighs approximately 20 g of marinated grilled meat on a bamboo skewer, representing a single-piece add-on portion as sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Satay, chicken, grilled — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) adapted for satay marinade and grilling
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists chicken satay per stick at approximately 35–40 kcal per stick (~20 g). Per-100g values derived from HPB Foodo chicken satay data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy grilled chicken (NDB 05013) adjusted for marinade contribution (sugar, spices). Values represent a composite chicken satay estimate. Beef and mutton variants exist but chicken is the most common reference form.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and marinade dishes — analogous estimate based on grilled marinated chicken (GI ~45) from University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore satay sticks. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on grilled marinated chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate from marinade sugars. GL per serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 x 0.84) / 100 = 0.38, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: One satay stick contributes approximately 64 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit total satay consumption and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as peanut sauce with added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content per stick
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 45 and only 0.84 g carbohydrate per stick, a single satay stick has minimal glycemic impact and can be included in a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though peanut sauce accompaniment should be accounted for separately.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and cholesterol from grilled meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: One satay stick provides approximately 0.56 g saturated fat and 15.6 mg cholesterol; grilled preparation without added oil is preferable to fried alternatives, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate total intake and choose chicken over beef or mutton variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A satay stick (one piece) is a single skewer of marinated grilled meat, a fundamental unit of satay — one of the most iconic dishes in Singaporean, Malaysian, and Indonesian cuisine. Each stick consists of small cubes or strips of meat (most commonly chicken, but also beef or mutton) threaded onto a thin bamboo skewer, marinated in a spiced paste of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, coriander, cumin, sugar, and salt, then grilled over hot charcoal until lightly charred and fragrant. The result is tender, smoky, and subtly sweet with aromatic spice notes. In Singapore, satay is a hawker staple sold by the stick, typically priced at SGD 0.50–2.00 per piece depending on the vendor and meat type. The dish is commonly served with a rich peanut dipping sauce, compressed rice cakes (ketupat), and sliced cucumber and onion, though these accompaniments are not included in the single-stick nutritional profile. Nutritionally, one satay stick (~20 g) provides approximately 37 kcal, 4.1 g protein, 1.9 g fat, and 0.84 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate snack or meal component. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), reflecting the dominant protein and fat matrix with minimal marinade sugars. Clinically, satay is a relatively lean protein source when grilled without added oil; sodium from marinade seasoning is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals. The food is halal-certified at most Singaporean hawker centres and is gluten-free in its base form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in one satay stick?
How much protein in one satay stick?
What is the GI of satay?
How many satay sticks in a serving?

confidence_reason: No exact per-stick nutritional entry found in HPB Foodo for a single satay stick sold as an add-on. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo chicken satay category data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy grilled chicken, adjusted for marinade. The 20 g per-stick serving size is a standard estimate for Singaporean hawker satay. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for grilled chicken satay. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this food. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for satay. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the cited sources. The input food name 'Additional One Sate Stick' reflects a field survey menu add-on label from Dapur Penyet - Funan; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Satay Stick (One Piece)'. Peanut sauce, ketupat, and cucumber accompaniments are not included in this nutritional profile. Nutrient values represent chicken satay as the reference variant; beef and mutton satay will differ in fat and cholesterol content.
